panels.description: Wide shot: A gloomy Victorian cemetery under a heavy, grey sky. In the foreground, a freshly dug grave with a tombstone reading 'JACOB MARLEY'. A few figures in black mourning clothes stand in the distance. The atmosphere is somber and cold.

bubbles.text: Marley was dead: to begin with. There is no doubt whatever about that.
The register of his burial was signed by the clergyman, the clerk, the undertaker, and the chief mourner.

panels.description: Medium shot: A close-up on a withered, skeletal hand holding a quill, signing 'Scrooge' in sharp, aggressive cursive on a dusty parchment burial register. A single candle flickers nearby, casting long, dark shadows across the desk.

bubbles.text: Scrooge signed it: and Scrooge's name was good upon 'Change, for anything he chose to put his hand to.

panels.description: Wide shot: A gritty, extreme close-up of a massive, rusted iron door-nail embedded in ancient, rotting wood. The focus is sharp on the texture of the rust. Dramatic side-lighting creates high contrast.

bubbles.text: Old Marley was as dead as a door-nail. You will therefore permit me to repeat, emphatically, that Marley was as dead as a door-nail.

panels.description: Medium shot: Scrooge sitting at a tall desk in his dark counting-house. He looks cold and indifferent, his face etched with lines of greed. In the background, a faded sign on the wall reads 'SCROOGE AND MARLEY'.

bubbles.text: Scrooge knew he was dead? Of course he did. Scrooge and he were partners for I don't know how many years.

panels.description: Medium shot: Scrooge standing at the edge of Marley's funeral in the rain. While others look sad, Scrooge is surreptitiously counting coins or inspecting a ledger, a predatory glint in his eye. He is the only mourner.

bubbles.text: Scrooge was his sole executor... and even Scrooge was not so dreadfully cut up, but solemnised it with an undoubted bargain.

panels.description: Wide shot: Scrooge walking down a narrow, foggy London alleyway at night. Gas lamps glow dimly through the mist. He is a solitary, hunched figure against the oppressive architecture of the city.

bubbles.text: This must be distinctly understood, or nothing wonderful can come of the story I am going to relate.

panels.description: Wide shot: A split composition. On one side, a faint, ghostly silhouette of a king on a castle battlement (Hamlet reference). On the other, the dark, empty interior of Scrooge's home. The air looks thick with dust motes and silence.

bubbles.text: If we were not convinced that Hamlet's father died, nothing would be remarkable. Marley's ghost was not to be seen.
